items.title: CVE-2026-34926: Trend Micro Apex One RCE Actively Exploited — CISA Sets June 4 Federal Patch Deadline
items.summary: A critical remote code execution vulnerability in Trend Micro Apex One has been added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. Federal civilian agencies have until June 4, 2026 to apply the vendor-supplied patch.
items.why: Active exploitation with a binding federal remediation deadline makes this urgent for any org running Apex One. The KEV listing signals real adversary use, not just proof-of-concept research.

items.title: CVE-2026-31431: Linux Kernel 'Copy Fail' Root Priv-Esc Added to CISA KEV — Public 732-Byte Python PoC
items.summary: CISA added CVE-2026-31431 to its KEV catalog — a logic bug in the Linux kernel's authentication cryptographic template (CVSS 7.8) that lets any unprivileged local user gain root via a 732-byte Python exploit. The PoC is publicly available and exploitation is confirmed in the wild.
items.why: A working public exploit for root on Linux is a high-signal event. Any multi-tenant Linux environment, container host, or shared server is at risk until the kernel is patched or the workaround applied.

items.title: APT28 (Fancy Bear) Weaponizes CVE-2026-21509 Within 24 Hours, Deploys Cloud C2 Against European Military & Gov Targets
items.summary: Trellix Research tracked a multi-stage APT28 espionage campaign targeting military and government entities across Poland, Slovenia, Turkey, Greece, the UAE, and Ukraine. The group weaponized Microsoft Office vulnerability CVE-2026-21509 within 24 hours of public disclosure and leveraged cloud infrastructure for command-and-control to blend with legitimate traffic.
items.why: 24-hour weaponization of a new Office CVE combined with cloud C2 demonstrates elite offensive cadence. Defense teams need to enforce Office macro/link policies and monitor cloud egress for anomalous C2 patterns now.

items.title: Mandiant M-Trends 2026: Attackers Hand Off Stolen Access in 22 Seconds; New AI-Evasive Malware Families PROMPTFLUX & PROMPTSTEAL Identified
items.summary: Mandiant's M-Trends 2026 report — compiled from 450,000 hours of incident response — reveals attackers are now handing off compromised access to secondary operators in as little as 22 seconds. The report also documents two novel malware families, PROMPTFLUX and PROMPTSTEAL, that query LLMs mid-execution to dynamically generate evasion logic, defeating static signature and behavioral detection.
items.why: 22-second handoff shatters the assumption that defenders have minutes to isolate an alert. PROMPTFLUX/PROMPTSTEAL represent a new class of AI-native malware that renders traditional detection models insufficient without LLM-aware behavioral analysis.

items.title: CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report: eCrime Breakout Now 29 Minutes; GenAI Weaponized at 90+ Organizations
items.summary: CrowdStrike's annual threat report documents a 65% speed increase in eCrime breakout times — now averaging 29 minutes, with the fastest observed at 27 seconds. Adversaries also injected malicious prompts into legitimate GenAI tools at over 90 organizations to automate credential theft and cryptocurrency exfiltration, and AI-enabled adversary activity increased 89% year-over-year.
items.why: Sub-30-minute breakout fundamentally challenges detection-and-response workflows calibrated for hour-scale dwell times. Organizations without automated containment playbooks are structurally behind the threat curve.
